chain_of_thought: Native crash occurs in Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum inside libUbikeyJni.so; stack shows pure app-native chain without allocator/sanitizer frames, but I cannot decompile due to MCP HTTP errors, so reasoning must be based on symbols and call sequence only.

Function names indicate a custom ASN.1/X.509 parser: CERT_Load -> Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT -> Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT -> Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum; crash reason string 'Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum' suggests either an abort or bad access during computation of the number of child elements in an ASN.1 structure.

Such functions typically walk ASN.1 sequences/sets based on length/offset fields read directly from untrusted certificate bytes; fuzz harness fuzz_one_input almost certainly feeds arbitrary certificate-like blobs into CERT_Load or the JNI, mimicking untrusted network or file certs in real app use (e.g., smartcard PKI, SSL validation).

Crashes in ASN.1 child-count computation are commonly due to missing bounds checks (e.g., trusting length fields, integer overflow, or loop index going out of bounds) leading to out-of-bounds reads/writes or invalid pointer dereferences; they are classic memory-safety bugs (cf. many historical ASN.1 vulns).

No evidence suggests this is a harness-only issue: all crashing frames are real app functions (no fuzzer glue inside lib, just an external fuzz_one_input); JNI bridge Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetPolicyOID suggests Java passes a certificate/policy blob down to native code without necessarily constraining ASN.1 structure beyond type; in production, those blobs come from external PKI or user tokens, i.e., attacker-influenced.

Termination description uses Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum as 'Process Termination' rather than a generic 'SIGABRT', but since this is the top frame it is consistent with a fault in that function; even if the function calls abort/exit on malformed ASN.1, untrusted input can still trigger repeated crashes (DoS) and likely indicates deeper parsing defects; with usual ASN.1 complexity, probability of memory corruption is high.

Given typical patterns and the cryptographic context, this is best classified as a likely memory-safety vulnerability, with high impact (potential code execution or at least denial-of-service) and realistic trigger via malformed certificate data controlled by an attacker (network MITM, rogue certificate, or malicious token).
reasons: Crash occurs inside custom ASN.1/X.509 parsing code (Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum) in app library, not in fuzz-only or allocator code.
ASN.1 child-count computations are historically prone to out-of-bounds and integer bugs when reading attacker-controlled length fields, and here they are driven by fuzzed certificate input.
JNI entry Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetPolicyOID implies native code processes external policy/certificate OIDs, making this path realistically reachable via untrusted input in production.
No signs the crash is due solely to harness misuse (no null JNI handles or impossible call pattern), pointing instead to intrinsic parser fragility.
recommendations: Manually audit Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum and surrounding ASN.1 parsing routines for length/offset validation, integer overflow, and bounds checks before dereferencing or looping.
Add robust defensive checks for all ASN.1 length and child-count fields, rejecting inputs that would move pointers beyond the backing buffer or exceed reasonable limits.
Run targeted fuzzing with ASan/UBSan enabled on CERT_Load/Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT to confirm and precisely characterize the memory safety issue and develop regression tests.
assumptions: CERT_Load and related functions parse ASN.1-encoded certificate or policy data directly from attacker-influenced sources (network, token, or file).
The crash in Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum is due to improper handling of ASN.1 structure/length rather than an intentional fatal assert used only in test builds.
Java side does not fully validate the ASN.1 structure before invoking the JNI method, leaving native parser exposed to malformed inputs.
limitations: MCP decompilation of libUbikeyJni.so failed (HTTP 404), so the exact instruction and argument flow at the crash site cannot be confirmed.
No Java call graph or source context was available to verify exact origin and validation of the input blob.
Allocator/sanitizer details and exact signal/exception type are not provided, limiting precision on whether this is OOB read, write, or abort-on-assert.

exploit.trigger_method: Malformed ASN.1/X.509 certificate or policy blob processed via UbikeyJni.jGetPolicyOID
prerequisites: Attacker can cause the app to process a chosen certificate or policy object (e.g., via network TLS endpoint, smartcard/USB token, or imported cert file).
UbikeyJni.jGetPolicyOID is reachable in the production app in a context that parses such external data.
exploit_pipeline: Craft an ASN.1/X.509 certificate or policy structure with inconsistent or extreme child count/length fields likely to break Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum.
Deliver this crafted object through the mechanism the app uses to obtain certificates (e.g., remote server certificate, imported certificate file, or token-provided cert).
Trigger the code path that calls UbikeyJni.jGetPolicyOID so that CERT_Load and subsequent ASN.1 parsers process the malicious structure.
Observe application crash or abnormal behavior; iterate on the ASN.1 structure using guided fuzzing to attempt controlled memory corruption or more reliable denial-of-service.
exploit.notes: Exact exploitation (e.g., control over RIP/PC) cannot be confirmed without disassembly, but ASN.1 length/child-count handling in custom C parsers has a long history of leading to exploitable memory corruption. This PoC focuses first on triggering reliable app crashes by feeding malformed certificates into the flow that ultimately invokes jGetPolicyOID.

chain_of_thought: Identify first app-level native frame below allocator/sanitizer: in the stack, crash is in __memcpy (CRT/allocator), next frame is Decode_DER_ASN1_ST inside libUbikeyJni.so, so that is the first application-level function.

Without concrete Ghidra decompilation output provided in the prompt, I must reason generically about common patterns: Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, Get_DER_Child, Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT, CERT_Load clearly form a custom ASN.1 / X.509 certificate parsing pipeline implemented in libUbikeyJni.so.

A memcpy-triggered crash during DER/ASN.1 decoding is highly indicative of an out-of-bounds read/write or size miscalculation. ASN.1 structures contain attacker-controlled length fields; if they are not strictly validated, the decoder may copy more bytes than the destination buffer size, directly causing a memcpy crash.

The call chain: fuzz_one_input -> Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetPolicyOID -> CERT_Load -> Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT -> Get_DER_Child -> Decode_DER_ASN1_ST -> __memcpy. The fuzz harness replaces real application inputs but uses the same JNI entry point as production Java, so the path is realistic and reachable when the app processes certificates/policies (e.g., from smart card, server, or file).

JNI entry Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetPolicyOID likely receives a byte[] or similar DER blob, passes it (with its length) into CERT_Load without strong bounds enforcement at the native level. Commonly, JNI code obtains a pointer and length via GetByteArrayElements/GetArrayLength and forwards them to the decoder; unless additional checks are implemented, the decoder fully trusts embedded ASN.1 lengths.

Given the specific function names (Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, Get_DER_Child), the most plausible design is an internal ASN.1 library that walks TLV structures, allocating or using fixed buffers and calling memcpy to copy child elements or OIDs. If the crash is in memcpy and there is no indication of harness-only misuse (e.g., NULL pointers or impossible Java-side constraints), the root cause is likely insufficient validation of length fields or buffer sizes inside these decoding functions.

This type of bug is a classic memory safety vulnerability (CWE-787 or CWE-119). Such issues in certificate parsing are frequently exploitable for out-of-bounds write, which can overwrite adjacent heap or stack data, potentially enabling code execution; minimally, it is a robust denial-of-service vector.

No evidence suggests the crash results from an artificial harness condition like passing NULL where app would never do so; instead, fuzzing just explored malformed cert inputs that a network attacker or untrusted file could supply. Thus, the issue should be treated as a genuine, externally-triggerable vulnerability in ASN.1/X.509 processing.

Without the actual decompiled code, I cannot precisely show the memcpy(src,dst,len) arguments or exact validation logic. However, the function naming, the memcpy crash, and the realistic JNI path together provide strong evidence of an unsafe copy driven by untrusted DER content. Therefore, classify as a likely vulnerability with high (but not absolute) confidence, severity high, mapping to out-of-bounds memory access during ASN.1 decoding.
reasons: Crash occurs in __memcpy called from custom ASN.1/X.509 decoding functions in libUbikeyJni.so, indicating a size/bounds error during copy.
ASN.1 DER length fields are attacker-controlled; insufficient validation commonly leads to out-of-bounds reads/writes in memcpy when parsing malformed certificates.

The crash path is via a real JNI bridge Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetPolicyOID into certificate loading (CERT_Load), so malformed external certificate/policy data can realistically reach this code.
No signs that the crash is purely a fuzz-harness artifact such as impossible NULLs or invalid JNI usage; instead it matches typical certificate parser vulnerabilities.
recommendations: Review and harden Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, Get_DER_Child, and related ASN.1 parsing code to validate all length fields before memcpy/memmove, ensuring destination buffer sizes are not exceeded.
Add structured negative tests and fuzzing for malformed DER/ASN.1 and X.509 inputs focused on length overflows and nested structures to reproduce and confirm the crash, then verify fixes.

Consider replacing custom ASN.1/X.509 parsing with a well-vetted library or at least centralizing bounds checking and using safer APIs (e.g., memmove with explicit size checks, checked vector operations).
assumptions: Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetPolicyOID accepts attacker-influenced certificate or policy blobs (from network, NFC, smartcard, or untrusted files).
The crash is due to an out-of-bounds access in memcpy rather than an artificial abort in the C runtime.
JNI/native glue does not fully sanitize ASN.1 structures and relies on the native decoder for validation.
limitations: Actual decompiled source of Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, Get_DER_Child, CERT_Load, and the JNI method is not provided, so memcpy arguments and exact bounds checks cannot be shown.
The precise origin of the certificate/policy data in the real app (network vs. local device) is unknown, so the exact threat model may vary.
Exploitability beyond denial-of-service (e.g., reliable RCE) cannot be confirmed without heap layout and allocator behavior analysis.

exploit.trigger_method: malformed X.509/ASN.1 certificate or policy data passed through jGetPolicyOID JNI
prerequisites: Attacker can cause the app to process an untrusted or modified certificate/policy (e.g., via network MITM, server compromise, or crafted file/token).
The vulnerable code path (UbikeyJni.jGetPolicyOID -> CERT_Load) is reachable under normal app use with external data.
ASLR and platform mitigations are present but may be bypassable with additional vulnerabilities or heap grooming.
exploit_pipeline: Craft an ASN.1 DER-encoded certificate or policy object whose internal length fields cause Decode_DER_ASN1_ST to compute an oversized memcpy length or invalid child offsets.
Deliver the crafted certificate/policy to the mobile app via whichever channel it uses for UbikeyJni.jGetPolicyOID (e.g., TLS-like handshake, smart card, or file import).
Trigger the Java code path that calls UbikeyJni.jGetPolicyOID, leading into CERT_Load and Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT in libUbikeyJni.so.

When Decode_DER_ASN1_ST processes the malformed structure, it invokes memcpy with out-of-bounds parameters, corrupting adjacent memory and crashing the app or enabling further memory manipulation for exploitation.
